speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I get used to the light. And then it would be dark here all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, then you have to put some lights here. To light the lights.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the sun is out, we get a lot of-- got a lot of sun. It's cloudy today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it's a nice day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I like it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I like when the sun is out, and it's not too hot. [LAUGHS] I don't like when it's too hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. May the 21st, 1995. The survivor is Sonia Friedman. Mary Rothschild interviewer. Los Angeles, California, USA, and it will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- that's the today. This is May 21, 1995. I am Mary Rothschild interviewing Sonia Friedman for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today the 21st?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: I need a tissue.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number one with Sonia Friedman. Please tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Epstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-P-S-Z-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 20, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a small town in Eastern Europe, Eastern Poland, excuse me, Wisniowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the town, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town. To me, at that time, it seemed like all the Jews lived in town and the non-Jews lived somewhere on the outskirts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The only interesting thing-- the name came because there were a lot of cherries, [NON-ENGLISH]. And from there, the name came, Wisniowiec. It had a very elaborate palace that the story was going that during-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember. Not [? Minitske, ?] one of the invaders, build it for his chief adjutant. He build this palace for him. Now, the palace was really the most beautiful thing in town with luscious gardens. With-- the Jews really couldn't come in there, as a rule. But later, the school where I was going when I was, like, a high school, they-- the school was there. They also had schools there, trade schools, for Poles. There weren't Jews there. And a lot of other things. But when I think of the town, I always think of the [INAUDIBLE], which was called the palace. We had friends. We had neighbors. And the whole street where I lived was my family. It seems like my grandfather had a lot of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trzeciego Maja, which means the 3rd of May, the day of independence Poland in 1918. Probably had a different name because this town was under Russia before I was born until 1918. And then it became Poland till 1939 when the Russians occupied it again. So in every house lived a relative. My father, his parents were married the second time and each one had children. They had a lot of children. It-- when-- when we left the town, I was thinking back, like, the whole length of this block then around the corner live-- lived all the relatives. And some of them lived in other countries like France and other countries, Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perez Epstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaia Epstein. her maiden name was Slep, S-L-E-P.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sisters, and I had one brother who died of complications of the war right after the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sonia, can you describe the home that you grow up in, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always think of a very sunny, pleasant place. When you think of in today's terms, it probably was nothing. But it was a bit of cheerful home with lace curtains and red floors and a lot of plants. And with loving parents. And our business was adjacent to the-- to the house. And always sisters, a brother, relatives. I told you that we had a lot of relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a business was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents had a business, but I remember when I was very young, I-- that we had, it was called [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know what it means, it's called here. But when I was getting a little older, we mainly had school supplies, books, and a lot of items that had to do with stationery that were gift things, photography. But the big part of it was school books and school supplies. There were a lot of villages, and they used all come to us and buy the-- for the schools. Like about 36 schools were around there. The only other store that was of the same was my father's sister and brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there to elementary school. After the elementary school, they opened an [INAUDIBLE], a secondary school like a high school. And I went there for about a year and a half. And after it, my parents sold everything and moved to big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Rowne. Because my brother was the oldest and he was going already to school in the next town. And then, they had to send me, and it was a lot of money to-- to live out and we had two more younger children. And my mother was never happy to live in a small town. And eventually, they-- first they rented the property for a bank. And then they sold it, and we lived in a different town, in Rowne. And we lived, 1939 when the war broke out, that's where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to think, if I moved in '37-- I think '37-- so I was 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- do-- what language were they speaking in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents spoke a lot of Russian between themselves. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They spoke Yiddish. But they spoke a lot of Russian between themselves. My mother really was-- she-- she used to teach, like, not in a public school, but she used to teach Russian. So-- and they both spoke Russian a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your favorite subjects were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, poetry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poetry?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- do you remember your dreams and aspiration? What did you want to become when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, when I left the town, I just was thinking in terms of finishing high school, gymnasium. I really couldn't see myself in a-- I wasn't-- I don't remember. If I did think of any professional of anything, I don't remember it. I was thinking of school, of friends, of activities that would go on with all this. But [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] our family had a lot of interaction with the Poles, with the [INAUDIBLE], Poles that lived there. And as a rule, they used to show a lot of respect to my parents, especially my mother. And I remember at one time came a chief of police, a new man to town. And I and his daughter became very close friends. She loved poetry. She was a dreamer. We used to go and talk. She had brothers that were so anti-Semitic, like the worst image of a hooligan that you can imagine. Those were her brothers. That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they were anti-Semitic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably the way they talked, the way they behaved.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I just remember the impression that I know they were terribly anti-Semitic, and I used to think that I was scared of them. But I used to love [? Janka, ?] that was her name. Now, it wasn't very popular to have a close friend that's not Jewish. I can't even remember how my parents reacted to what happened. But since he was like a career man, he didn't stay too long there. And he left, I think before-- they left before I left. Another time, I remember when Pilsudski died. And those I remember are the times of anti-Semitism. Pilsudski died. My girlfriend was wearing, like a sweater that was the color of brick. And everybody had to wear, at the school, a black band on their arm. And some guys attacked her because either she dared to wear red when the country is in such-- and we were crying for Pilsudski. We learned poems. We were heartbroken when he was-- but that was the attitude which I remember. I also remember that Pole-- the Poles and then I was older already-- that the Poles came out, that they shoot, concentrate to get a business in Polish hands. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that Poles should buy from Poles. Now, this is-- so across the street from us, they opened a business, the same as ours. And put in, like, a cut out of a human-sized person that it said was the slogan, 'buy only from a Polack.' And I guess this really made us to-- made my parents to start to think about moving away somewhere else because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '36, '35, I don't remember. I could be younger. I don't think so. They didn't last more than a year. They-- but that was-- then the Ukranian in our town were very nationalistic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, we heard Ukranian talk in a language that was-- that the Poles probably would listen to it. They used to leave on consignment, paintings. Very good paintings, the Ukranian. They usual-- they used to leave a lot of-- but they had those national-- national teams of them. About their heroes like Shevchenko. And the Ukranians, we were scared more than the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Poles considered themselves sort of aristocracy, blue blood. Now, the Ukrainians, everybody knew were the peasants. But that probably was my impression, but that probably wasn't true. They probably had between-- because you can see today, they have a country and everything. But in my mind, they were the peasants. Anyway, we left the city, and we went to big-- live in a big down. We have relatives there. And my father completely had a new way of making a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was doing business with Lodz. It was the center of textile. He was doing business when he had a business. So they gave him salesmanship of a territory mostly in the area where we used to live. So he had to travel. And he used to buy out in closeouts, like big, like, truckloads or wagons of, like, some items like yarn or something else. And he used to have a place where we had like a warehouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while he was selling it, the merchandise from that company, which was completely something else, was like clothing, needed stuff, he was selling the yarn and carrying the-- and carrying the notes himself. And they paid him. I mean, it was like pulling teeth. But that's how he made a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we sold our property, and what we both was not as expensive, so he had capital to-- to-- to use it. So that's how he made a living. Now, when the Russians came, if you had a business, you were considered bourgeoisie and they would send you to Siberia. But he had a cousin that used to-- that had a hotel, and they send him to Siberia. But his son-in-law took it over and employed my father in some kind of a job by the desk. And he had the status of a workman, and we had no trouble with authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sonia, what was-- was religion observed in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house was very traditional, very traditional. I mean, kashrut and holidays was very observant. My father used to go to shul like Friday night and Saturday. My mother I remember used to complain always that my father is not religious enough. Constantly, she used to complain that he wasn't religious enough. But nothing like the background that you heard before. We didn't have Hasidic Jews living in this town. And like I'll never forget that a piece of bread was found just before Passover on the step between our store and the house. Our housekeeper [? some-- ?] it was her fault. And my mother was, I'll never forget. She was in hysterics that-- so you can imagine that that was, I mean, we didn't mix any foods. We were-- my mother used to go on the holidays to shul, but she used to say [INAUDIBLE] in the house. So that's the kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Shabbat dinner in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday night was a big thing. We had always dinner. And I remember my brother would not always show up in time. And I took it personally to go to drag him, wherever he was. He should sit at the table on Friday night. And as he get older, he sometimes hid a book somewhere. And I-- and it was very disturbing to me that he does. That he-- he makes trouble for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Shabbat-- but Shabbat was different than, like, if you hear from a Orthodox Jew. My father used to go to shul. My mother was a very delicate woman, very not healthy. She had a lot of problems when I remember when I was young.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to have the permit to go to that palace and to the park anytime we want, the family. So we used to go for a whole day. And we didn't live far from there. I remember running home because I forgot something. But you had to pass a little-- there was a-- a little area that was very wooded, like [INAUDIBLE]. And that they said somebody's crazy there. I remember running like crazy just to pass this thing. But it was a beautiful place there. And we used to spend the days. We used to go to the river to bathe and to swim on Shabbos. And we used to have company after lunch. They used to come visiting or my parents went visiting. That's how we-- and when I got older, we used to gather-- get together at the end of Shabbat with friends and wait till it gets dark so we can do the things that we want to do. We also had influence from Zionist organizations. And I was involved in an organization which was not-- not on the extreme right and not on the extreme left, but rather middle of the road. And we used to get together and sing songs and talk about Palestine and have discussions. And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the relationship between the Jews and non-Jews in the city you moved to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very interesting. The main street was a very, not like in that small town, was a very big street. It really crosses the town. And every evening, that was the thing, the youth went out to-- for walks and used to meet each other. On one side of the street, the Jews were congregating and on the other side, the non-Jews were congregating. And it seems like if you don't go on the other side of the street, you thought you're all beating Jews. I mean, from different-- this city had a lot of gymnasiums, different. They had the Hebrew. They had a [INAUDIBLE] gymnasium. They had a Polish gymnasium. They had a Russian gymnasium. They had a gymnasium that had-- they had like also schools that you could learn how to sew and all kind. Anyway, so all that youth used to come out. They used to pick their beauty. They used to have all kind of things going. They had organizations too, but I never got to it. I was not involved. I had one friend that came once to visit us when I was in the small town, and she was wearing a uniform. And I was very close with her, and when I came, but she wasn't involved anymore in it, too. Maybe we were getting older. And I didn't want to go through this, like, going [INAUDIBLE] and working. And it's so hard. Really, what I wanted was to finish high school and to work in one of those nice buildings, the banks or something like that. But it would never, ever happen by the Poles because they weren't taking any Jews. I worked there, but the Jews-- but it was by the  Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if you had any close friends in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, in this small town, we had very close friends. I mean, we used to-- we used to talk about dreams, what we expect in life. We used to laugh. And we used to-- and when I left, everybody gave me pictures, and we were crying and were-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And then in this city, I made friends. Not-- I had a few friends that were close, like this girl that-- I-- I made some close friends, but the memory's not as deep as-- of the time that where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sonia, what is your happiest memory from the times before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to love Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I use to love all that baking and cooking. And my father, before, probably, I was born, was doing business with [INAUDIBLE] used to buy the porcelain. And we had the most gorgeous porcelain and crystal and-- and dishes and those tureens and candlestick. And they used to be all hidden all the year. And for Passover, they used to take it out. And a lot of it was on display. And then my friends used to come, I used to pretend that we having a tea party, and I used to take-- I used to love the holidays. I used to love the holidays. Look through the window and see the girls with new shoes wearing the dresses. Going with her father to the synagogue. I mean, [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I used to like a lot of things. I used to love to be in the store. I used to love when school started. I-- my brother never liked it, couldn't drag him there. But I used to love business. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What aspects of business did you lie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just what I liked when I had a toy store. [LAUGHS] Maybe use some creativity to find something or to do something with the things that we had. I used to love this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your lives change when Hitler invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first place, I'll never forget that the first Passover was no Passover. My father was in the hotel working all night. And we were sitting, and there was not nothing. We were all just sitting and waiting for him. And he probably didn't show up till somewhere in the middle of the night. There was no holidays. Now, my brother went to school to another town because he finished high school. And he went to Lwow. So I didn't see my brother. I mean, he used to come in whenever he did in trouble. He burned his hands in some kind of chemistry. And my mother didn't have anybody to help her in the house. She was very-- and we had a lot of refugees. My mother used to cook a storm. They used to come, all the people that-- we lived, like, when the main street, you went into a huge court. The court had a lot of people living there. And she used to be like a caretaker, like if you get the key to the-- and then when you cross the court from the other side, there was a small street with two duplexes. I don't remember there were more than 10. And that's where we lived. And we had like a garden with trees. And we used to kid around that I was very popular because of the garden. Even if I wasn't home, nobody cared. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's not what I started to tell you. What did you-- excuse me. What was the question? How were your lives affected--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, affected?

speaker: interviewer
text: --by the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the school, I-- I lost interest. I had to learn subjects that I wasn't interested. Now, I used to think that since my parents spoke Russian, that I'd have no problem. But of course, I-- I didn't say one word that was grammatically correct. And I really had to learn. But learning Russian was not as bad. Really, I was bored with the subjects, with history of the party and work and profit and all the subjects from Karl Marx. All this. And then we had different hours to go to school. So I-- I really didn't have such a big incentive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father through somebody he knew, got me a job in an office till I-- till 12 o'clock. And learned bookkeeping. And I liked it. Because I-- I had a very nice handwriting and I loved to keep everything. And at first, I didn't know much. But it seems like of little I knowed, I was always getting recognition. It was very-- I liked it very much. How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this was 1940. I was 19 years old. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we had all those people coming in to us. And they didn't live all with us. We took in one family and some single man that for awhile stayed with us. But that wasn't bothering me. I mean, comfort, like with other things that are important today, didn't mean much to us. And my mother all of a sudden was very strong. And as I said, she used to make meals. And a lot of those refugees were having the meals with us. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I still remember preparing for Yom Kippur. Everybody was in shul when I and my girlfriend were going from one house to the other one. And we were fasting and preparing all that good food on the table. We knew we can't touch it. But I saw that all our friends are not religious anymore. I saw them eating on Yom Kippur. I was working on Yom Kippur. I had to come into the office. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a while, it really shook me up. I mean, all that approach that-- that-- religion is the opium of the masses and that everything-- and the theory of Darwinism is God and all that stuff. Life was not as happy. And-- but as far as things to get, we weren't lacking too much because we had relatives that-- on the small towns and they used to send us everything. All kind of-- and it wasn't kosher anymore. They used to send us salamis and all kind of meats and all kind of stuff.
